videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Seattle and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do in and around the city. I actually grew up in this area so it's going to be really interesting to visit some of the spots that I saw as a child. So enjoy this video. We are Zac and Ine, full-time digital nomads from the United States and Belgium. We met while studying abroad in 2018 and have been traveling the world together ever since. We're here to inspire your next adventure, whether it's hiking in the Canadian Rockies, a road trip through the U.S., or backpacking across Africa. On our channel, you'll find unfiltered videos of hiking guides and the best things to do in destinations around the world. Seattle is located in the northwest of Washington and is the state's largest city. It's nicknamed the "Emerald City" because the center and surrounding areas are filled with greenery year-round. And despite its reputation for rainy days, Seattle is not an indoorsy place. The city is packed with everything: from beautiful parks, to iconic landmarks, and waterfront tourist attractions. But it's also a great hub for exploring the nearby mountains, islands, and volcanoes. In this video, we'll highlight the best things to do in and around Seattle. With professional sports teams and a famous grunge music scene, you can catch a game or enjoy a concert in the city. Watch the Mariners play baseball at T-mobile park or head to the nearby Lumen Field to watch the Seattle Seahawks football team or Sounders FC soccer team. And if you're more into music than sports, then join a concert at the Show Box or visit the Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP). So this is Climate Pledge Arena. It used to be where my favorite NBA Basketball Team played, the Seattle Supersonics. But they're not here anymore unfortunately, but they do have the Seattle hockey team and they also have the Women's professional basketball. The ninth best thing to do in Seattle is to walk the bustling waterfront piers. There are tons of seafood restaurants, souvenir shops, and tourist attractions here. You can take in the views from the Seattle Great Wheel, watch the Seattle sea life at the Aquarium, go on a whale watching tour, enjoy a virtual reality flight at the Wings Over Washington, and finally taste some local seafood at the famous Ivar's restaurant. At number eight we have skiing or hiking at Snoqualmie Pass. Located about 50 miles to the east of Seattle, Snoqualmie is an easy and quick getaway from the city. Snoqualmie Pass has beautiful mountain scenery making it the perfect place to go skiing in the winter and hiking in the summer. Our favorite hiking trail in the area is the Snow Lake Trail, which is roughly six miles round trip and takes you to a beautiful, crystal clear alpine lake. Your visit to snoqualmie pass can also be combined with a quick stop at the Snoqualmie Falls. The Puget Sound is dotted with tranquil islands away from the hustle and bustle of Seattle. Some of them are just a short ferry ride away from downtown. We recommend visiting my hometown of Vashon Island, which is known for its pristine beaches, hiking trails, shops, art galleries, and restaurants. You can take a passenger only ferry from Pier 50 during the weekdays. However, it's actually better to explore the island by car since there's not a great public transportation infrastructure there. If you take your own car, you can board the ferry from the Fauntleroy Ferry Dock in West Seattle, which usually leaves every 30 to 60 minutes every day of the week. A visit to Seattle isn't complete without visiting one of the lush parks, which offer a great taste of the Northwest landscape. The largest and most beautiful park in the city is Discovery Park, located about five miles north of the downtown area. The park is home to varied landscapes such as tidal beaches, open meadows, dramatic sea cliffs, and thick forests. Other beautiful parks include Green Lake and Alki Beach Park. So we're here at Discovery Park now, taking a nice break from the city. And we're doing the Discovery Loop Trail, we'll put the map on the screen so you can see what the route looks like. And behind us there, you can see Vashon Island. The fifth best thing to do in Seattle is to admire the skyline. The Seattle skyline is one of the most iconic ones in all of the U.S. From the world famous Space Needle to the oldest skyscraper of Smith Tower, and the tallest viewing area at Columbia Tower. However, if you're looking for a free viewing platform that is at least as impressive then head to Kerry Park. Here, you'll see the Space Needle in the foreground with the city skyline and Mount Rainier in the back. The Seattle Space Needle is the most iconic landmark in all of Seattle. It was built for the 1962 World's Fair and has been a modern marvel of architecture ever since. Located at Seattle Center, the Space Needle stands at 605 feet tall and you can see it up close from the park around it. But besides this icon, Seattle Center is also home to a wide variety of other attractions and museums, such as the Chihuly Garden and Glass and the Pacific Science Center. The third best thing to do in Seattle is to take an Underground Tour. This unique tour takes you through subterranean storefronts and sidewalks which were buried when the city rebuilt itself after the great fire of 1889. The guided tour lasts about 75 minutes and starts near the historic Pioneer Square. It's a humorous and fun way to learn about Seattle's history. We weren't allowed to take videos underneath the city, but check out some of the pictures from our experience. You can book your own tour via the link in the description below. Mount Rainier is the most scenic day trip that you can do from Seattle. Located about two to three hours from Seattle, this volcanic peak is the centerpiece of the National Park, which covers more than 240,000 acres of forest. There are multiple entrances to the park, but the Nisqually Entrance to the southeast of the park is the most popular one thanks to its year-round access and its close proximity to Seattle. It's a great place for hiking as well as snowshoeing in the winter. Our favorite day hike in the park is the Tolmie Peak Lookout Trail, which can be accessed via the Mowich Lake gravel road. Getting to Mount Rainier can be hard without your own vehicle, so we've included a day tour link in the description below as well. So we made it to Tolmie Peak lookout and there's the Fire Lookout right there actually. so we just passed it, we found ourselves a really incredible spot to eat lunch. Like it's all a ridge around us so you can actually keep walking a little bit if you want. And we have an incredible view here of Eunice Lake. At number one we have Pike Place Market. Pike Place is one of the best atmospheres in all of downtown. Every minute there are thousands of people walking through the market and taking in the scene. From local crafts to produce and music, the Pike Place market has something for everyone. Grab a bite to eat for lunch here and make sure to stop at the first Starbucks ever. After the market, you can also visit the Gum Wall at Post Alley. This unique but kind of gross spot has literally millions of pieces of gum that have been stuck by tourists. Seattle is a bustling city and of course there are things to do that we haven't mentioned in this video, such as the Seattle Free Walking tour, the hip neighborhood of Ballard, the student area of Capitol Hill, and Lake Washington. However, after visiting the spots in the city that I used to go to 10 years ago, I definitely saw a difference in how Seattle's downtown has changed. Unfortunately, there's a lot more homelessness than in the past and it doesn't feel as safe to walk around the areas, like Pike Place or Pioneer Square. So if you're visiting those areas, stay alert and stay safe. Other than that, you'll have a great time visiting Seattle. We hope you enjoyed this video and we'll catch you on the next adventure.

videos.title: Perfect Day in Seattle Without a Car: 14+ Stops in the Emerald City
videos.description:
In this video, we’ll take you through the perfect day in Seattle, all without needing a car! From the vibrant Pike Place Market to the quirky Gum Wall, we’ll start with iconic sights before indulging in a delicious meal at Lola Restaurant. We’ll then explore Seattle’s must-visit attractions like the Space Needle, Chihuly Garden, and the Museum of Pop Culture. After a coffee break at Café Vita, we’ll stroll through the Olympic Sculpture Garden and cap the day with dinner at Von’s 1000 Spirits, a ride on the Seattle Great Wheel, and drinks at the Inside Passage Bar. Join us for a full day of Seattle’s best spots and let us know what we left off in the comments!

videos.title: A Drive Through The WORST Neighborhoods In Seattle, Washington
videos.description:
What is happening in Seattle these days??

For this video, I went to the worst part of Seattle, Washington. If you dig around online and ask people who live in Seattle, it’s pretty clear where the worst parts of Seattle are located. It’s all in an area on the south side of Seattle here, in an area that surrounds the King County Airport. This is what is called South Seattle, and the areas I’ll through are South Park, Rainier Beach, White Center and Beacon Hill.

It was a dreary day in late October, and as soon as I got off the highway and made my way towards the first neighborhood, immediately, I saw streets filled with homeless people. This is something you see over Seattle. All of a sudden you turn a corner and there’s a bunch of people living on the streets.

However, most of what we’ll see for this video won’t be a lot of homeless camps. You can learn about Seattle’s massive homeless camps in another video I did. For the most part, the areas we’ll see are all neighborhoods without homeless encampments. 

We’re going to begin in Beacon Hill, located here, just off of I-5, about 5 miles south of downtown. 

Now you’re gonna hear me observe throughout this video - the worst parts of Seattle are not that bad visually. There aren’t blocks after blocks of boarded up homes and houses in disrepair like you would see in many other large cities. Perhaps that’s because of how valuable real estate is here, and how expensive Seattle is. There’s no real cheap housing in greater Seattle, as I’m sure you’ve heard. However, there’s lots of poverty, drug use, gang activity and crime in the areas we’ll visit. I wouldn’t be here if that wasn’t the case.

Here in Beacon Hill, and many of the other neighborhoods we’ll drive through, crime and poverty are both some of the highest in the city of Seattle. Overall, violent crime in Seattle is higher than the national average, but far less than in places like Chicago. It’s about as violent here as it is in Jacksonville or Louisville. The big problem in Seattle is property crime. Seattle is the 14th worst place in the nation for thefts, robberies and breakins. And a lot of that has to do with the large number of homelessness here and the lack of police enforcement. 

If you’re curious, for perspective, a home here in Beacon Hill costs about $730,000. Yes, and hopefully that helps you realize how expensive Seattle is. This neighborhood isn’t a disaster by any means, but $730,000?

Now we’re going to travel to another one of Seattle’s worst neighborhoods, to a part of town called White Center. It’s located way down here, west of state route 509. This place has the nickname Rat City because there were rats everywhere down here at one one in this neighborhood’s history. It’s cheap here, which means it’s really diverse. The average home price here is $575,000, which is far less than in Beacon Hill. That’s a steal for Seattle. Here in White Center, you have a 1 in 23 chance of being the victim of a crime. That actually makes it far safer than in Beacon Hill, where folks have a 1 in 14 chance of being victimized. At night, White Center gets really shady with car breakins and shootings. The roads are old and damaged and businesses have trouble staying open for long periods. 

Our next stop on our bad Seattle hood tour is Rainier Beach, a part of town well known to many people in Seattle. There’s about 12,000 people in this neighborhood, located on the southern edge of town. This is actually the second most dangerous hood in Seattle. Crime here is 158% higher than the national average. Everyone here has a 1 in 14 chance of being victimized every year.  It’s not Georgetown bad, but it’s bad. It’s not uncommon to hear gunshots in this part of town at night. It definitely has what you could call a vibrant street culture. It’s a poorer working class neighborhood, but this part of town is slowly being gentrified. 

For our last stop and final worst neighborhood, we’re going to visit South Park, which is surrounded by a large industrial area on the south side of town. To me, this neighborhood looked the least rundown, but this is also the most dangerous neighborhood in Seattle. Crime here is nearly 200% higher than the national average, and there’s a big problem with stolen cars down here in South Park. People have a 1 in 11 chance of being a victim of some type of crime every year. Homes here are the cheapest of all - at just over $500,000 each as of this writing.

First time in Seattle or back for more? Explore the Emerald City’s most distinctive neighborhoods, packed with waterfront views, iconic landmarks, hidden trolls, and unforgettable scenery.

videos.transcript: Visiting Seattle for the first time or coming back for more? The Seattle neighborhood travel guide will help you experience the best of the city — from the skyline to the shoreline. Get ready to explore the historic streets and waterfront of Downtown, the iconic views of Lower Queen Anne, and the maritime heritage of Ballard. We'll also explore the quirky avenues of Fremont, the relaxed beachside vibes of West Seattle, and the scenic charms of Bainbridge Island. Join us as we discover the iconic landmarks, hidden gems, and laid-back hangouts that make the Emerald City one of a kind. Downtown Seattle blends big, bold architecture, historic streets, and waterfront views. Packed with attractions and things to do, it's the neighborhood where locals and visitors cross paths every day. You'll find eye-popping art and design throughout Downtown. And nowhere shows it off quite like the Seattle Central Library. This striking glass-and-steel marvel feels more like a modern art installation. Just a 15-minute walk away, step into the bustle and aromas of Pike Place Market. Founded in 1907, it's a maze of giant crab claws, fresh-cut flowers, and endless stalls filled with local produce, eats, and crafts. Time for a coffee break? Across the street is the original Starbucks, the roster that helped make Seattle one of the coffee capitals of the world. A few steps away is Post Alley, home to the city's stickiest landmark, the infamous Gum Wall. Post Alley is more than just gum, though. It has plenty of great places to hang out, shop, and refuel. From Pike Place, step out onto Overlook Walk, which connects Downtown to the Central Waterfront. Hit the walk’s promenade where you'll find restaurants, open air hangouts, and attractions like the Seattle Great Wheel on Pier 57. Round out your waterfront visit at Victor Steinbrueck Park, the perfect spot to sit back, relax, and take in sweeping views of Elliot Bay and beyond. From the waterfront, you'll be within minutes of one of Seattle's oldest neighborhoods, Pioneer Square. It's a great place to soak up some of Seattle's history. And for a peek into the future, head to the Seattle Spheres. This trio of glass domes in the Denny Triangle is part of Amazon's campus in the South Lake Union neighborhood near Downtown. Inside, employees work and meet among more than 40,000 plants from tropical cloud forests around the world. Okay, that's the big one done. Now, let's head uptown to our next neighborhood. Hop aboard the Seattle Monorail at the West Lake Center, which connects Downtown to our next neighborhood, Lower Queen Anne and its star attraction, the Seattle Center. Towering over this 74-acre cultural and entertainment hub is Seattle's most iconic landmark, the Space Needle. Ride the elevator 520 ft up to the observation deck, where glass floors and panoramic views put the entire city at your feet. On a clear day, Mount Rainier and the Olympic Mountains frame a city surrounded by sea and sky. At its base, the cool architecture continues at the Museum of Pop Culture, also known as MoPop, and Chihuly Garden and Glass. Both are bold and colorful, even from the outside. For the perfect Lower Queen Anne finale, it's just a quick drive or 25-minute walk to Kerry Park. From this little overlook, the city unfolds before you with the Space Needle front and center, Elliot Bay shimmering below, and on clear days, the snowcapped peak of Mount Rainier rising in the distance. Northwest of Downtown Seattle, Ballard blends salty sea air with relaxed old-world charm. Start at the Ballard Locks and Fish Ladder, where nature and engineering meet in epic ways. Watch boats being lifted and lowered as they make their way between Puget Sound and Lake Union And check out the viewing windows to see salmon make their way upstream. Just down the road, meet Frankie Feetsplinter, Ballard’s resident troll. You'll find him right outside the Nordic Museum, which celebrates the neighborhood's Nordic roots. On Sundays, the Farmers Market takes over Ballard Avenue, with local produce, artisan goods, and live music, creating a true community vibe. The street itself, lined with historic buildings, is filled with bars, restaurants, and indie shops. And vinyl lovers will already know all about Sonic Boom Records, one of America's best record stores. Ballard is also home to some of Seattle's best outdoor escapes. Golden Gardens Park offers sandy beaches, forest trails, and volleyball courts. All with sweeping views of the Sound and beyond. And when the sun starts to set, head to Sunset Hill Park and join locals for the best show in town. Right next door to Ballard is the neighborhood of Fremont. If you're into creative, quirky vibes, this one's for you. Fremont is home to the city's most famous troll of all. You'll find him lurking under the Aurora Bridge, where he's been welcoming visitors since the 1990s. For a dose of nature with a steampunk twist, head to Gas Works Park, a former industrial plant turned artsy, urban green space. From its grassy hills, you'll get sweeping views over Union Lake, the Downtown skyline, and the Space Needle. It's a local's favorite for picnics, sunsets, and watching boats drift by. Stop by Fremont Vintage Mall for retro treasures, or pop into Portage Bay goods for the kinds of gifts and keepsakes you won't find anywhere else. If you thought Fremont was laidback, wait until you see our next neighborhood. For an even slower pace and wide open skies, head across Elliot Bay to West Seattle. Take the water taxi from Downtown for a scenic ride across the bay. The neighborhood stretches out with beachy village vibes. Kick off at Alki Beach, a sandy stretch lined with seafood shacks and ice creamies. It's the perfect spot for kayaking, biking, or just doing nothing at all. Nearby, Hamilton Viewpoint Park offers another spectacular perspective with fairies and ships gliding past the city. Spend a morning on California Avenue. And if you've got room in your luggage for more vinyl, head to Easy Street Records, a renowned local institution that's also a cafe and live music venue. When it's time to unwind, head to Lincoln Park, where driftwood beaches, forest trails, and saltwater pools serve up yet another slice of Pacific Northwest perfection. You might even spot another troll. Just across the water is another of Seattle's neighborhood gems. For the perfect day trip, head Downtown to Pier 52 and catch a ferry to Bainbridge Island. For 35 blissful minutes, you’ll sail across Puget Sound, with views of Mount Rainier to the south, the Olympic Mountains to the west, and the skyline behind you. After you hop off at Eagle Harbor, spend a couple of hours on Winslow Way, the island's main street. It's lined with boutiques, bookstores, and cute shops filled with cool furnishings and local art. With its blend of coastal beauty, creative spirit, and that incredible ferry crossing, Bainbridge Island is the perfect place to wind down after the bustle of the city. From the soaring heights of the Space Needle to the driftwood covered shores of West Seattle, to every cobblestone and cafe in between, Seattle has a neighborhood to match every mood and style. So, pack your bags, bring your appetite and your sense of adventure, and get ready to fall in love with Seattle, one neighborhood at a time. Don't forget to share this video with your travel buddies, because Seattle's always better when it's enjoyed with friends.
